text_blocks.text: [[i class="fa-solid fa-list"]][[/i]]Contents
[[a href="#section1"]]The Julia set[[/a]]
[[a href="#section2"]]The Mandelbrot & Julia connection[[/a]]
[[a href="#section3"]]Further reading[[/a]]
The Julia set

In the previous section we showed how the Mandelbrot set can be generated using the expression
                    @@math:8:e9b2c0@@
                    This is a particular case of the quadratic recurrence equation
                    @@math:7:56d901@@
                    with @@math:9:1ff6bc@@ a fixed complex number. The set we obtain with this equation is known as the Julia set.
                    In fact, there is a different Julia set for almost every @@math:10:7a924f@@

Similarly as we did for the Mandelbrot set, we obtain a sequence of complex numbers
                    @@math:11:4cab1f@@ with @@math:12:0f1dae@@ Again, the points @@math:13:7d93c1@@ are said to form the orbit of @@math:14:a38589@@
                    and the Julia set is defined as follows:

If the orbit @@math:15:90c67a@@ fails to escape to infinity, the initial @@math:16:5cefd4@@ is said to belong to
                        the [[em]]filled-in Julia Set[[/em]].

The Julia set is named after the French mathematician [[a
                        href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaston_Julia" target="_blank"]]Gaston Julia[[/a]]
                    who investigated their properties in 1915 and culminated in his famous paper in 1918:
                    [[a href="http://sites.mathdoc.fr/JMPA/PDF/JMPA_1918_8_1_A2_0.pdf" target="_blank"]][[em]]Mémoire sur
                            l'itération des fonctions rationnelles[[/em]][[/a]].
                    While the Julia set is now associated with the quadratic polynomial in (\ref{julia}),
                    Julia was interested in the iterative properties of a more general expression, namely
                    @@math:17:efeee6@@
                    The Julia sets, defined by the equation (\ref{julia}), can take all kinds of
                    shapes, and a small change in @@math:18:abe329@@ can change the Julia
                    set very greatly. In 1979, with the help of computer, B. B. Mandelbrot studied the Julia sets
                    and tried to classify all the possible shapes and came up with a new shape: the Mandelbrot Set.

Explore the Julia sets in the applet below. Zoom in or out in different regions. Change the number of
                    iterations
                    and observe what happens to the plot. Move the mouse around and observe the different Julia sets
                    depending
                    of the value of @@math:19:fd7af2@@
The Mandelbrot & Julia sets connection

Due to the definition of the Mandelbrot set, there is a close correspondence between the geometry
                    of the Mandelbrot set at a given point and the structure of the corresponding Julia set. In other
                    words, the Mandelbrot set forms a kind of index into the Julia set.
                    A Julia set is either connected or disconnected, values of @@math:20:1d0427@@ chosen from within the
                    Mandelbrot set are connected while those from the outside of the Mandelbrot
                    set are disconnected. The disconnected sets are often called [[em]]dust[[/em]],
                    they consist of individual points no matter what resolution they are viewed at.

Explore the relationship between the Mandelbrot and Julia sets in the following applet. Move the
                    mouse over to the Mandelbrot set to observe different Julia sets. Zoom in or out in different
                    regions.
                    Open the Controls menu to change the number of iterations or choose an specific value of @@math:21:ff3321@@

[[strong]]Note:[[/strong]] This applet was recently included in some of the animations for the following video
                    from [[a href="https://www.quantamagazine.org/" target="_blank"]]Quanta Magazine[[/a]]:

[[strong]]Article:[[/strong]] [[a
                        href="https://www.quantamagazine.org/the-quest-to-decode-the-mandelbrot-set-maths-famed-fractal-20240126/"
                        target="_blank"]]The Quest to Decode the Mandelbrot Set, Math's Famed Fractal [[i
                            class="fas fa-external-link-alt"]][[/i]][[/a]]
Further reading
The applets were made with [[a href="https://p5js.org"]]p5.js[[/a]] and the source code can be found:

[[a href="https://github.com/complex-analysis/complex-analysis.github.io/tree/master/applets/p5js/julia"
                            target="_blank"]]Julia set[[/a]]

[[a href="https://github.com/complex-analysis/complex-analysis.github.io/tree/master/applets/p5js/julia-mandelbrot"
                            target="_blank"]]Julia-Mandelbrot relationship[[/a]]
If you want to learn how to program it yourself, I recommend you this tutorial:

[[a href="https://thecodingtrain.com/CodingChallenges/022-juliaset.html" target="_blank"]]Coding
                            Challenge: Julia set[[/a]]

Finally, I recommend you two of the most widely read basic introductions to the Mandelbrot and Julia sets:
  • [[a href="https://www.springer.com/gp/book/9783540158516" target="_blank"]]The Beauty of Fractals[[/a]] by Heinz-Otto Peitgen & Peter H. Richter, Munich: Springer-Verlang, 1986.
  • [[a href="https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-1-84996-486-9" target="_blank"]]The Colours of Infinity,[[/a]] by Nigel Lesmoir-Gordon (ed), London: Springer-Verlang, 2010.
They both cover other fascinating fractals and contain many spectacular pictures.
Conformal Mapping[[i class="fa-solid fa-angles-right"]][[/i]]

@@verbatim:0:7f2533@@

Contents

  • The Julia set
  • The Mandelbrot & Julia connection
  • Further reading
    • @@verbatim:1:3225cb@@


The Julia set

In the previous section we showed how the Mandelbrot set can be generated using the expression @@math:8:e9b2c0@@ This is a particular case of the quadratic recurrence equation @@math:7:56d901@@ with @@math:9:1ff6bc@@ a fixed complex number. The set we obtain with this equation is known as the Julia set. In fact, there is a different Julia set for almost every @@math:10:7a924f@@

Similarly as we did for the Mandelbrot set, we obtain a sequence of complex numbers @@math:11:4cab1f@@ with @@math:12:0f1dae@@ Again, the points @@math:13:7d93c1@@ are said to form the orbit of @@math:14:a38589@@ and the Julia set is defined as follows:

If the orbit @@math:15:90c67a@@ fails to escape to infinity, the initial @@math:16:5cefd4@@ is said to belong to the filled-in Julia Set.

The Julia set is named after the French mathematician Gaston Julia who investigated their properties in 1915 and culminated in his famous paper in 1918: Mémoire sur l'itération des fonctions rationnelles. While the Julia set is now associated with the quadratic polynomial in (\ref{julia}), Julia was interested in the iterative properties of a more general expression, namely @@math:17:efeee6@@ The Julia sets, defined by the equation (\ref{julia}), can take all kinds of shapes, and a small change in @@math:18:abe329@@ can change the Julia set very greatly. In 1979, with the help of computer, B. B. Mandelbrot studied the Julia sets and tried to classify all the possible shapes and came up with a new shape: the Mandelbrot Set.

Explore the Julia sets in the applet below. Zoom in or out in different regions. Change the number of iterations and observe what happens to the plot. Move the mouse around and observe the different Julia sets depending of the value of @@math:19:fd7af2@@

@@verbatim:4:b1982f@@
Control keys:
+ (Zoom In)
- (Zoom Out)
R (Reset view)
I (Info & Frame)
  

The Mandelbrot & Julia sets connection

Due to the definition of the Mandelbrot set, there is a close correspondence between the geometry of the Mandelbrot set at a given point and the structure of the corresponding Julia set. In other words, the Mandelbrot set forms a kind of index into the Julia set. A Julia set is either connected or disconnected, values of @@math:20:1d0427@@ chosen from within the Mandelbrot set are connected while those from the outside of the Mandelbrot set are disconnected. The disconnected sets are often called dust, they consist of individual points no matter what resolution they are viewed at.

Explore the relationship between the Mandelbrot and Julia sets in the following applet. Move the mouse over to the Mandelbrot set to observe different Julia sets. Zoom in or out in different regions. Open the Controls menu to change the number of iterations or choose an specific value of @@math:21:ff3321@@

@@verbatim:5:246181@@
Control keys:
+ (Zoom In)
- (Zoom Out)
R (Reset view)
I (Info & Frame)
@@verbatim:2:1bc474@@
  

Note: This applet was recently included in some of the animations for the following video from Quanta Magazine:

@@verbatim:6:9c8abe@@

Article: The Quest to Decode the Mandelbrot Set, Math's Famed Fractal

Further reading

The applets were made with p5.js and the source code can be found:

  • Julia set
  • Julia-Mandelbrot relationship

If you want to learn how to program it yourself, I recommend you this tutorial:

  • Coding Challenge: Julia set

Finally, I recommend you two of the most widely read basic introductions to the Mandelbrot and Julia sets:

  • The Beauty of Fractals by Heinz-Otto Peitgen & Peter H. Richter, Munich: Springer-Verlang, 1986.
  • The Colours of Infinity, by Nigel Lesmoir-Gordon (ed), London: Springer-Verlang, 2010.
They both cover other fascinating fractals and contain many spectacular pictures.
